title: Multiboxing Setup Guide
subtitle: By Sarah Marathai
body:
# How to setup for multiboxing

---

This guide is going to walk through some of the core elements of multiboxing effectively in TLA fleets. It will go through utilizing third party tools, UI optimization, overview settings and some general tips that will help you get started multiboxing. By the end of the guide, dualboxing accounts should feel as efficient and effective as single boxing.


Let's get started by setting up the Eve-O Preview tool. Almost every multiboxer uses this tool to improve the speed and control of their clients. You may also use Eve-X Preview if that is your preference, though setup steps may vary.


# __Setting up Eve-O Preview__


1. First, download Eve-O Preview (Or Eve-X if you prefer.)\
Download page for Eve-O Preview (Version 6.0.1.3)\
https://github.com/Proopai/eve-o-preview/releases/tag/v6.0.1.3 \
Main Github page for full documentation:\
https://github.com/Proopai/eve-o-preview \
__(Note: I use the 6.0.1.3 version of Eve-O and have never updated to any future releases. This is up to your preference, use the Github page and check out all releases should you choose to update.)__


2. Unzip and install after downloading. (make sure to Unzip to a folder outside of the Program files or Program Files (x86) or it may not work properly.)

3. After Installing, run the Eve-O Preview.exe and also open up your Eve clients. (This will create a new Eve-O Preview.json file in the folder with the .exe)

4. Once the exe is running, you can use the executable to change the look and feel of the preview windows for each of your clients.

5. There is no “best way" to set up the preview windows, so play around with the settings to find out what works best for you with your screens and clients.  

(Example Eve-O Preview setup)

![](unnamed.png) ![](unnamed__1_.png)
  

For Example, I have it off to the side of my screen like this:                                                                         ⬇
![](unnamed__2_.png)

# Creating a cycle key in Eve-O Preview


1. After setting up your Eve-O Preview.exe, a new Eve-O Preview.json file should appear in the same folder as the Eve-O Preview.exe. (Always keep the .json file in the same folder as the exe.)

2. At this point, close all Eve clients and the Eve-O Preview.exe if you have them open. (Make sure Eve-O is actually closed, it likes to go minimized to the system tray.)

3. Open the .json file. (it should open in a notepad or similar txt file editor.)

4. The new file should look like this at the top: \
![](unnamed__3_.png)       

5. Start by selecting your preferred cycle order for your clients. This order will tell Eve-O which client you want to come next each time you use the cycle key. Change the text “Example DPS toon 1” to the full name of the Eve Character (not account name) you want it to select. \
For example, I have told Eve-O to use this order: Kaitlyn → Kasumi → Sarah \
![](unnamed__4_.png)   
__(Note: You can add more than 3 clients to this group by simply adding new lines that follow the same format. “EVE - Example DPS toon 4”: 4, Make sure the syntax stays consistent and watch out for missing commas, and semicolons.)__

6. Next, you want to change the cycle key config to your preferred hotkey for cycling. Think about using a key that will be easy to press while flying your ships. Keys that are close to the F1-F4 keys are recommended. \
For example, here, I have changed the cycle forward key to respond to “1”, and the cycle backwards key to respond to “2”. Pressing 1 will now move it to the next client, and 2 will move it back to the previous client. \
![](unnamed__12_.png)  \
(Note: Whatever key you are using will continue to function as its normal function. I.e. “1” will still type a “1” into chats and activate any hotkeys assigned to it. Find a key on your keyboard that is convenient that you don't mind not being able to use.)


7. After making these changes to the .json file, make sure to manually save the file in the same folder as before. (Note: If you have any Eve clients or the Eve-O Preveiw.exe open during this process it may not work properly and you will have to try again.)

8. Open up Eve on all of your clients and also run the Eve-O Preview.exe

9. Try using the cycle key you saved in the .json file to test if it is working properly. Sometimes certain keys are finicky and this might take a few tries to get it working the way you want it.

Obviously, this guide does not show you all the potential options and you can set up your preview UI however it feels comfortable to you. Explore around in the options for Eve-O preview and figure out the best set up for your comfort and effectiveness.

# Eve Overview / UI optimizations for Multiboxing


In TLA fleets, the goal of every dualboxer should be nearly 100% effectiveness when compared to a single boxer. This requires practice and hard work to maximize your effectiveness but lets try and make it a little easier through optimizations.


One of the best ways to improve your multiboxing speed and efficacy is to minimize the effort required to navigate your UI interface. This this best achieved by:


- __Reducing mouse movement__
- __Eliminating unnecessary clicking__
- __Using hotkeys and shortcuts__
- __Simultaneous mouse and keyboard usage__

In that effort, here are some ways you can improve your UI for maximum efficiency using multiple clients.  


## General UI advice


Ultimately, your UI settings are up to personal preference and there is no one “best” way to layout your interface. It can be helpful to take a few minutes to think about your UI layout. Give some consideration to your UI elements and ask yourself what is really important while boxing:


- __Are the elements spaced too far apart?__

- __Do you use some parts more often than others?__

- __Are certain elements too large compared to their importance?__

- __Are D-scan, the map, or unnecessary chats taking up space?__

- __What does each part of the UI do to help me fly effectively?__


For me, I play on a small screen, so screen space is at a premium. This means I have taken measures to maximize the space I have for efficiency. As you can see here. \
![](unnamed__5_.png)




The UI elements are spaced closely together to minimize the amount of movement from my mouse and maximize screen space. For example, the broadcast window is directly next to the overview window and the selected item box is right above that. Everything is stacked tightly together, off to one side and set to maximum opacity. For my set up and screens, this has given me the best UI clarity and effectiveness for my style. Think about how your UI is set up to compliment your playstyle.


This same setup is replicated across all of my clients in exactly the same way. The UI is pixel perfect from one client to the next. For example, these 4 of my clients are almost identical, except for the modules on each of the ships.
![](unnamed__6_.jpg)![](unnamed__7_.jpg)
![](unnamed__8_.jpg)![](unnamed__9_.jpg)



Utilizing the power of your Eve-O preview (as we set up before), you can use the cycle key to switch clients quickly and take the same action on all your clients in quick succession.


If all of your clients are stacked on top of each other with pixel precision, you will not have to move your mouse at all to take the same action on each of your clients.


For example, if you want to align your ships to a broadcast, you can use the align hotkey (a by default), cycle client hotkey, align hotkey, cycle client hotkey, etc. Till all the clients are aligning. Without having to move your mouse at all.


## Pixel Stacking


As I mentioned in the previous section, setting up your client pixel stacked on top of each other can help to improve your boxing performance.


1. In the in-game Eve settings, under display and graphics, you will want to change your Eve clients to either fixed window or windowed mode, so that it is no longer full screen. (Playing in full screen mode may cause issues with Eve-O preview, your experience could vary.)

2. Next, change the window size and UI scaling to be the same settings on all of your clients. (This again is personal preference but try to keep all of your clients consistent.) Move the client windows on top of each other so that they are all aligned perfectly.

3. Now exit the setting menu and select a “main” client you wish to be your primary model. This client will be the template for all your other clients.

4. Take a moment and adjust your main client’s UI to your preference. Once you feel it is perfect, close all of your Eve clients and download the Eve settings manager tool: \
Download page for Eve Setting Manager: \
https://github.com/mintnick/eve-settings-manager/releases \
Main Github page for full documentation: \
https://github.com/mintnick/eve-settings-manager


__(Note: This is not the only tool that can do this, and there are others if you dislike this one, I just found this one the simplest and easiest to use.)__


5. Unzip and install. Then, run the Eve Settings Manager.exe.

*After running the Eve Settings Manager.exe it will look something like this:*

![](unnamed__10_.png)

*Characters Settings*   ↑                                                                              *Account Settings*  ↑

                        
6. You may not have any characters or accounts listed when you first open up the settings manager. If so, you will need to manually tell the app where to look for your settings using the open folder/select folder at the top of the app.

7. The Eve settings data will typically be in your program files, search around if you can not find it at first. Try looking in these folders for your settings information: \
C:\Users\%username%\AppData\Local\CCP\EVE\c_program_files_(x86)_eve_sharedcache_tq_tranquility\settings_Default \
Or: \
C:\Users\%username%\AppData\Local\CCP\EVE\c_eve_sharedcache_tq_tranquility\settings_Default

8. Once the settings data has populated, Select the character of which you wish to copy the settings. (The character that you chose in step 3). The character will be highlighted in blue. This character’s specific settings will be copied over to all of your other characters, __make sure it is the correct one before proceeding.__

9. Click Overwrite all if you want __ALL__ of your clients to be overwritten or use the overwrite menu to only select specific characters. (make sure all your Eve clients are closed or this may not work properly.)

10. Now repeat steps 8 and 9 but this time with the account settings.

__(Note: This step can be somewhat tricky, as it is difficult to tell which account ID number belongs to which account. The easy way to tell the accounts apart is to use the timestamp next to the account ID. This timestamp updates whenever an Eve client is closed. Open your main account, then close the account again to update the time stamp.)__ \
![](unnamed__11_.png)


Once you have done this, close the Eve settings manager and open all of your Eve clients. They should now have the exact same settings and positioning for all of the UI elements in the game.

The UI for your Eve clients should now be pixel perfect and you should be able to use your Eve-O preview to cycle through clients that are perfectly pixel stacked on top of one another.
